title: Daily Market Digest: March 14, 2026
subtitle: Geopolitical Tensions Weigh Heavily on Global Markets, Oil Prices Surge

items.headline: US Stocks Lose Ground Amid Iran War Fallout, Heading for Third Straight Weekly Loss
items.summary: US stock indexes are losing ground in afternoon trading Friday, with major indexes set for their third consecutive weekly loss, as the ongoing war with Iran continues to pressure oil prices and destabilize the global economy. Overall market sentiment reflects rising panic and uncertainty. Not financial advice.

items.statContext: Down 0.61% for the day, down 3.1% year-to-date.

items.headline: Ulta Beauty ($ULTA) Plunges on Weak Forecasts
items.summary: Ulta Beauty slid 14.24% today, leading S&P 500 decliners after the beauty retailer's latest quarterly results fell short of analysts' profit targets and provided soft full-year profit and comparable sales forecasts. Selling, general, and administrative expenses increased by 23%.

items.headline: Adobe ($ADBE) Drops Amid CEO Departure and Revenue Forecast Concerns
items.summary: Software maker Adobe fell 7.58% following news that its CEO plans to step down after 18 years and investors were underwhelmed by the company's forecast for recurring subscription revenue, despite beating sales and profit forecasts.

items.headline: Meta Platforms ($META) Declines on AI Model Delay Reports
items.summary: Meta Platforms declined 3.83% amid media reports indicating a delay in the rollout of a new artificial intelligence model due to performance concerns.

items.headline: Charles Schwab ($SCHW) Rises
items.summary: Financial services firm Charles Schwab saw its stock rise by 1.8% today, amidst a challenging market.

items.headline: Eli Lilly ($LLY) Adds Gains
items.summary: Eli Lilly, a pharmaceutical giant, added 1.3% as health care companies showed strength in today's trading.

items.headline: Philip Morris International ($PM) Climbs
items.summary: Philip Morris International gained 1.8%, with consumer goods companies performing relatively well.

items.headline: Iran War Drives Oil Prices Above $100, Fuels Inflation Concerns
items.summary: The ongoing war with Iran continues to be a dominant factor in markets, pushing Brent crude above $100 per barrel (up 1.5% to $101.95, up ~40% for the month) and U.S. crude to $98.03 (up 2.4%, up ~46% for the month). The conflict has disrupted traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, with analysts warning prices could reach $150 if blockades persist. This surge in energy costs is heightening inflation fears globally.

items.headline: Technology and Materials Lag, Utilities and Consumer Staples Lead in Choppy Market
items.summary: Six of the 11 primary S&P 500 sectors ended in the red today, with technology and materials leading the laggards, dropping 1.29% and 1.04% respectively. In contrast, defensive sectors like utilities (up 0.94%) and consumer staples (up 0.54%), along with financial services and healthcare, showed relative strength.

items.headline: Key Economic Data Signals Slowdown: GDP Revised Down, Consumer Sentiment Falls
items.summary: Recent economic data indicates a sharpening slowdown. The fourth-quarter real GDP growth rate was revised down to 0.7% from 1.4%, and preliminary March consumer sentiment dropped to its lowest level this year. January's PCE price index rose 0.3%, with the core PCE up 0.4%. The 10-year Treasury yield climbed to 4.28%.

items.headline: AI Investment Cycle Remains Critical Pillar for S&P 500 Earnings
items.summary: Despite market turbulence, the artificial intelligence (AI) investment cycle continues to be a crucial driver for S&P 500 earnings. Massive capital expenditures from 'hyperscalers' are flowing into other S&P 500 constituents, creating a growth loop. However, companies need to demonstrate tangible productivity gains for this cycle to be sustainable.

items.headline: Attention Turns to Next Week's Fed Meeting as Rate Hold Expected
items.summary: Market participants are looking ahead to next week's Federal Reserve policy meeting, where the central bank is widely expected to keep interest rates steady amidst persistent inflation concerns and a slowing economy.
